stars.name: Bodu
stars.description: 95 Herculis, a G2 giant at 128 parsecs notable as a visual binary with a striking color contrast. Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by the Maldives.

stars.name: Pincoya
stars.description: A K2 star in Sagittarius at 41 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Chile. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Alnasl
stars.description: Gamma-2 Sagittarii, a K0 giant at 30 parsecs marking the tip of the arrow in Sagittarius. Its name means 'the arrowhead' in Arabic.

stars.name: Ramus
stars.description: 102 Herculis, a B2 subgiant at 281 parsecs in Hercules. It is a hot, luminous blue star in the northern sky.

stars.name: Polis
stars.description: Mu Sagittarii, a B2 giant at extreme distance in the top of the Sagittarius 'Teapot' asterism. It is a complex multiple system whose components span a wide range of spectral types.

stars.name: Kaus Media
stars.description: Delta Sagittarii, a K3 giant at 107 parsecs forming the middle of the Archer's bow. Its name means 'the middle of the bow' in Arabic-Latin hybrid.

stars.name: Alasia
stars.description: A G5 dwarf in Serpens at 43 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Cyprus. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Tusizuo
stars.description: 109 Herculis, a K2 giant at 36 parsecs in Hercules. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism.

stars.name: Kaus Australis
stars.description: Epsilon Sagittarii, the brightest star in Sagittarius at magnitude 1.79, a B9.5 giant at 44 parsecs. It marks the base of the Archer's bow and lies near the direction of the Milky Way's center.

stars.name: Fafnir
stars.description: 42 Draconis, a K2 giant at 97 parsecs in the circumpolar constellation Draco. Named after the dragon from Norse mythology, it hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Kaus Borealis
stars.description: Lambda Sagittarii, a K1 giant at 24 parsecs forming the top of the Archer's bow. Its name means 'the top of the bow,' and it is the closest of the main Sagittarius stars.

stars.name: Vega
stars.description: Alpha Lyrae, the fifth brightest star in the sky and one of the most extensively studied stars in astrophysics, an A0 dwarf at 7.7 parsecs. It served as the original photometric standard (magnitude 0), has a prominent debris disk, and was the first star other than the Sun to be photographed and have its spectrum recorded.
notable_features: Former pole star, future pole star (~13,700 CE)
Prototype for the Vega photometric system (mag = 0)
Debris disk suggesting possible planetary system

stars.name: Struve 2398 A
stars.description: A K5 red dwarf at 3.6 parsecs in Draco, one half of a wide binary system of red dwarfs. It is among the nearest stellar systems to the Sun.

stars.name: Struve 2398 B
stars.description: The K5 red dwarf companion to Struve 2398 A at 3.5 parsecs. Together they form one of the closest binary systems to the Solar System.

stars.name: Xihe
stars.description: A G8 star in Lyra at 139 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by China after the solar goddess. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Ross 154
stars.description: A nearby M3.5 flare star at 3.0 parsecs in Sagittarius, one of the closest stars to the Sun. It produces frequent energetic flares despite being only 17% of the Sun's mass.

stars.name: Sheliak
stars.description: Beta Lyrae, the prototype of the Beta Lyrae class of semi-detached eclipsing binaries at 295 parsecs. Mass transfer between its components creates a thick accretion disk, making it one of the most studied interacting binary systems.

stars.name: Ainalrami
stars.description: Nu-1 Sagittarii, a K1 bright giant at 345 parsecs in Sagittarius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the eye of the archer.'

stars.name: Nunki
stars.description: Sigma Sagittarii, the second brightest star in Sagittarius, a B2.5 dwarf at 70 parsecs. It has one of the oldest known star names, appearing in Babylonian records, and it marks the handle of the Teapot asterism.

stars.name: Kaveh
stars.description: A G8 dwarf in Serpens at 127 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Iran after the mythological blacksmith. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Alya
stars.description: Theta-1 Serpentis, an A5 dwarf at 47 parsecs in the tail of Serpens Cauda. It forms a close visual pair with its twin Theta-2 Serpentis.

stars.name: Alya B
stars.description: Theta-2 Serpentis, the A5 companion to Alya at 52 parsecs. The pair form a beautiful matched double in small telescopes.

stars.name: Sulafat
stars.description: Gamma Lyrae, a B9 giant at 190 parsecs and the second brightest star in Lyra after Vega. Its name means 'the turtle' in Arabic, an early name for the constellation.

stars.name: Ascella
stars.description: Zeta Sagittarii, an A3 subgiant at 27 parsecs forming the base of the Teapot asterism. It is a close binary with a 21-year orbital period.

stars.name: Okab
stars.description: Zeta Aquilae, an A0 dwarf at 25 parsecs in the body of the Eagle. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the eagle.'

stars.name: Meridiana
stars.description: Alpha Coronae Australis, the brightest star in Corona Australis (the Southern Crown), an A0/A1 dwarf at 38 parsecs. Despite being the alpha star, it is only magnitude 4.1 in a faint constellation.

stars.name: Albaldah
stars.description: Pi Sagittarii, an F2 giant at 156 parsecs in the Teapot of Sagittarius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the town,' referencing a star group.

stars.name: Altais
stars.description: Delta Draconis, a G9 giant at 30 parsecs in the body of the Dragon. It is one of the nearer giant stars visible from northern latitudes.

stars.name: Aladfar
stars.description: Eta Lyrae, a B2.5 subgiant at 426 parsecs in the constellation Lyra. It is the faintest of the named stars in this small but prominent constellation.

stars.name: Gumala
stars.description: An F8 dwarf in Sagittarius at 28 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Indonesia. It hosts one of the first known 'hot Jupiter' exoplanets.

stars.name: Belel
stars.description: A K0 giant in Sagittarius at 111 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Senegal. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Arkab Prior
stars.description: Beta-1 Sagittarii, a B9 dwarf at 96 parsecs forming the front leg of the Archer. It is an optical pair with Arkab Posterior, the two appearing close in the sky but at different distances.

stars.name: Sika
stars.description: A G1 dwarf in Sagittarius at 58 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Togo. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Arkab Posterior
stars.description: Beta-2 Sagittarii, an F2 giant at 41 parsecs forming the rear leg of the Archer. Despite sharing the Beta designation with Arkab Prior, it lies at a significantly different distance.

stars.name: Rukbat
stars.description: Alpha Sagittarii, a B8 dwarf at 56 parsecs marking the Archer's knee. Despite being the alpha star, it is only the 15th brightest star in the constellation.

stars.name: Anser
stars.description: Alpha Vulpeculae, an M0 giant at 91 parsecs and the brightest star in the small constellation Vulpecula the Fox. Its Latin name means 'the goose,' a relic of the constellation's original name 'Vulpecula et Anser.'

stars.name: Albireo
stars.description: Beta Cygni A, one of the most celebrated double stars in the sky, a K3 giant at 133 parsecs. Its striking gold-and-blue contrast with its B8 companion makes it a favorite telescopic target.

stars.name: Albireo B
stars.description: The B8 blue companion to Albireo at 123 parsecs, providing the spectacular color contrast with the golden primary. Whether the two form a true binary remains debated.

stars.name: Uruk
stars.description: An F8 dwarf in Sagitta at 118 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Iraq after the ancient Sumerian city. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Alsafi
stars.description: Sigma Draconis, a K0 dwarf at just 5.8 parsecs in Draco and one of the closest solar-type stars. It has been a frequent target for SETI searches and exoplanet surveys.

stars.name: Campbell's Hydrogen Star
stars.description: A Wolf-Rayet star in Cygnus surrounded by a ring nebula of ejected hydrogen, one of the most visually striking planetary nebulae. Despite its 'star' designation, it is actually the central star of a nebula exhibiting powerful stellar winds.

stars.name: Sham
stars.description: Alpha Sagittae, a G0 bright giant at 130 parsecs and the brightest star in the tiny constellation Sagitta the Arrow. Its name means 'the arrow' in Arabic.

stars.name: Fawaris
stars.description: Delta Cygni, a B9.5 giant at 51 parsecs forming the west wing of Cygnus the Swan. It is a triple system where the primary will become the 'North Star' around 11,250 CE due to precession.

stars.name: Tarazed
stars.description: Gamma Aquilae, a K3 bright giant at 121 parsecs flanking Altair on one side. Its Persian name means 'the beam of the scale,' and its deep orange color contrasts with Altair's white.

stars.name: Altair
stars.description: Alpha Aquilae, the 12th brightest star in the sky at magnitude 0.76, an A7 main-sequence star at just 5.1 parsecs. One of the fastest-rotating bright stars known, it spins so rapidly that it is 14% wider at the equator than at the poles, a shape directly imaged by interferometry.
notable_features: Part of the Summer Triangle asterism

stars.name: Libertas
stars.description: Xi Aquilae, a K0 giant at 56 parsecs in Aquila hosting a confirmed exoplanet. Named 'freedom' in Latin through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign.

stars.name: Alshain
stars.description: Beta Aquilae, a G8 subgiant at 14 parsecs and the third brightest star in Aquila. Despite its Beta designation, it is notably fainter than Altair and Tarazed.

stars.name: Terebellum
stars.description: Omega Sagittarii, a G3/G5 giant at 26 parsecs forming part of a small quadrilateral asterism in Sagittarius. It is one of the closer named stars in this direction.

stars.name: Cygnus X-1
stars.description: One of the strongest X-ray sources in the sky and the first widely accepted stellar-mass black hole candidate, a B0 supergiant at 595 parsecs with an unseen companion of about 21 solar masses. Its identification as a black hole in the 1970s famously resolved a scientific bet between Stephen Hawking and Kip Thorne.

stars.name: Tianfu
stars.description: Tau Aquilae, a K0 giant at 142 parsecs in Aquila. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism meaning 'the celestial raft.'

stars.name: Antinous
stars.description: Theta Aquilae, a B9.5 giant at 88 parsecs in the now-defunct constellation Antinous that was merged with Aquila. It is a spectroscopic binary.

stars.name: Chechia
stars.description: A G5 star in Aquila at 66 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Tunisia. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Algedi
stars.description: Alpha-2 Capricorni, a G6/G8 giant at 32 parsecs forming a wide optical pair with Alpha-1. Both stars are themselves doubles, making the system appear as a wide pair to the naked eye.

stars.name: Alshat
stars.description: Nu Capricorni, a B9 subgiant at 78 parsecs in the constellation Capricornus. Its name means 'the sheep' in Arabic.

stars.name: Dabih
stars.description: Beta Capricorni, a complex multiple star system at 100 parsecs consisting of at least five components. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the lucky star of the slaughterer.'

stars.name: Sadr
stars.description: Gamma Cygni, a luminous F8 supergiant at 562 parsecs at the center of the Northern Cross asterism in Cygnus. It is surrounded by the Sadr region, a complex of emission nebulae including IC 1318.

stars.name: Peacock
stars.description: Alpha Pavonis, a B2 subgiant at 55 parsecs and the brightest star in Pavo the Peacock. Its English name was assigned by the Royal Air Force for navigational use.

stars.name: Aldulfin
stars.description: Epsilon Delphini, a B6 giant at 101 parsecs in the small constellation Delphinus. It forms part of the distinctive diamond-shaped asterism of the Dolphin.

stars.name: Rotanev
stars.description: Beta Delphini, an F5 subgiant at 31 parsecs whose name is 'Venator' spelled backward -- a prank by the astronomer Niccolao Cacciatore, whose Latinized surname is Venator.

stars.name: Sualocin
stars.description: Alpha Delphini, a B9 dwarf at 78 parsecs whose name is 'Nicolaus' reversed -- the companion prank to Rotanev, both inserted into the Palermo star catalog by Cacciatore in 1814.

stars.name: Deneb
stars.description: Alpha Cygni, the brightest star in Cygnus and one of the most luminous stars visible to the naked eye, an A2 supergiant at 433 parsecs. With roughly 200,000 times the Sun's luminosity, it forms the Summer Triangle with Vega and Altair, and marks the tail of the celestial Swan.
notable_features: One of the most luminous stars visible to the naked eye (~200,000 L_sun)

stars.name: Aljanah
stars.description: Epsilon Cygni, a K0 giant at 22 parsecs forming one wing of Cygnus the Swan. Its name means 'the wing' in Arabic.

stars.name: Albali
stars.description: Epsilon Aquarii, an A1 dwarf at 64 parsecs in Aquarius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the swallower.'

stars.name: Musica
stars.description: 18 Delphini, a G6 giant at 75 parsecs hosting a confirmed exoplanet. Its Latin name meaning 'music' was chosen through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign.

stars.name: Polaris Australis
stars.description: Sigma Octantis, the southern pole star, an F0 giant at 86 parsecs. At magnitude 5.45, it is barely visible to the naked eye and far less useful for navigation than its northern counterpart.

stars.name: Solaris
stars.description: A K5 star in Pegasus at 48 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Kitalpha
stars.description: Alpha Equulei, the brightest star in the smallest traditional constellation Equuleus the Little Horse, a G0 giant spectroscopic binary at 58 parsecs. Its name comes from a phrase meaning 'the section of the horse.'

stars.name: Lacaille 8760
stars.description: An M1/M2 red dwarf at 3.9 parsecs in Microscopium, the brightest red dwarf in the sky. Despite being among the Sun's nearest neighbors, it is invisible to the naked eye.

stars.name: Alderamin
stars.description: Alpha Cephei, an A7 subgiant at 15 parsecs that will become the North Star around 7500 CE due to precession. It is a fast rotator and one of the closer A-type stars.

stars.name: Alfirk
stars.description: Beta Cephei, the prototype of the Beta Cephei class of pulsating variable stars, a B2 giant at 210 parsecs. Its small, regular brightness variations over a 4.57-hour period define this important class of stellar pulsators.

stars.name: Sadalsuud
stars.description: Beta Aquarii, the brightest star in Aquarius, a G0 supergiant at 165 parsecs. Its name means 'the luckiest of the lucky,' reflecting ancient astrological associations with good fortune.

stars.name: Bunda
stars.description: Xi Aquarii, an A7 dwarf at 55 parsecs in Aquarius. Its name comes from an Aboriginal Australian word.

stars.name: Sāmaya
stars.description: An IAU-named exoplanet host star whose name means tranquility in Sanskrit. Named through the NameExoWorlds initiative.

stars.name: Nashira
stars.description: Gamma Capricorni, an A7 giant at 48 parsecs whose name means 'the bearer of good news' in Arabic. It is a suspected Delta Scuti variable.

stars.name: Azelfafage
stars.description: Pi-1 Cygni, a B3 subgiant at 529 parsecs in Cygnus. Its elaborate Arabic name may mean 'the horse's foot.'

stars.name: Bosona
stars.description: A K0 star in Aquarius at 194 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Bosnia and Herzegovina. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Garnet Star
stars.description: Mu Cephei, one of the largest and most luminous red supergiants known, an M2 supergiant at extreme distance. William Herschel named it for its deep crimson color, and as a semi-regular variable it represents a late stage of massive stellar evolution.

stars.name: Enif
stars.description: Epsilon Pegasi, a K2 supergiant at 211 parsecs marking the nose of the winged horse Pegasus. It is the brightest star in the constellation and occasionally displays flare-like brightenings.

stars.name: Deneb Algedi
stars.description: Delta Capricorni, an A5 metallic-line eclipsing binary at 12 parsecs. It is the brightest star in Capricornus and one of the nearest eclipsing binaries to the Sun.

stars.name: Aldhanab
stars.description: Gamma Gruis, a B8 giant at 65 parsecs and the third brightest star in Grus the Crane. Its name means 'the tail' in Arabic.

stars.name: Itonda
stars.description: A G2 dwarf in Grus at 46 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by the Republic of Congo. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Kurhah
stars.description: Xi Cephei, an Am metallic-line star at 30 parsecs in Cepheus. It is a double star with a faint companion.

stars.name: Sadalmelik
stars.description: Alpha Aquarii, a G2 supergiant at 161 parsecs and the second brightest star in Aquarius. Its name means 'the lucky star of the king' in Arabic.

stars.name: Alnair
stars.description: Alpha Gruis, the brightest star in Grus the Crane, a B7 subgiant at 31 parsecs. Its name simply means 'the bright one' in Arabic.

stars.name: Biham
stars.description: Theta Pegasi, an A2 dwarf at 28 parsecs in the constellation Pegasus. Its name derives from Arabic meaning 'the young livestock.'

stars.name: Ancha
stars.description: Theta Aquarii, a G8 giant at 57 parsecs in Aquarius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the hip.'

stars.name: Lang-Exster
stars.description: Alpha Tucanae, the brightest star in Tucana the Toucan, a K3 giant at 61 parsecs. Its somewhat unusual name derives from a historical form used in early southern hemisphere catalogs.

stars.name: Sadachbia
stars.description: Gamma Aquarii, an A0 dwarf at 50 parsecs in Aquarius. Its name means 'the lucky star of hidden things' in Arabic.

stars.name: Lionrock
stars.description: A G8 subgiant in Aquarius at 131 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Hong Kong after the Lion Rock landmark. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Kruger 60
stars.description: A nearby M2 dwarf binary at 4.0 parsecs in Cepheus, one of the closest star systems to the Sun. Both components are flare stars, and the system has a very high proper motion.

stars.name: Stellio
stars.description: Alpha Lacertae, an A1 dwarf at 31 parsecs and the brightest star in the faint constellation Lacerta the Lizard. Its Latin name means 'the starred lizard.'

stars.name: Situla
stars.description: Kappa Aquarii, a K2 giant at 66 parsecs in Aquarius. Its Latin name means 'the water jar,' describing the vessel poured by the Water Bearer.

stars.name: Homam
stars.description: Zeta Pegasi, a B8.5 dwarf at 63 parsecs in the body of Pegasus. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the whisperer.'

stars.name: Tiaki
stars.description: Beta Gruis, the second brightest star in Grus, an M5 giant at 54 parsecs with a vivid red color. Its Maori name was given through the IAU initiative and relates to the concept of guardianship.

stars.name: Matar
stars.description: Eta Pegasi, a G2 giant at 66 parsecs in the constellation Pegasus. It is a spectroscopic binary whose name derives from the Arabic for 'the fortunate rain.'

stars.name: Babcock's star
stars.description: HD 215441, a remarkable A0 peculiar star in Lacerta with the strongest magnetic field of any non-degenerate star, measuring about 34 kilogauss. Discovered by Horace Babcock, it is a unique laboratory for studying extreme stellar magnetism.

stars.name: Sadalbari
stars.description: Mu Pegasi, an M2 giant at 33 parsecs in Pegasus. Its name means 'the lucky star of the splendid one' in Arabic.

stars.name: Skat
stars.description: Delta Aquarii, an A3 dwarf at 49 parsecs and the third brightest star in Aquarius. It lies near the ecliptic and the radiant of the Delta Aquariid meteor shower.

stars.name: Tengshe
stars.description: A K5 supergiant in Lacerta at 490 parsecs. Its Chinese name references the 'Celestial Serpent' asterism in traditional Chinese astronomy.

stars.name: Helvetios
stars.description: 51 Pegasi, a G5 dwarf at 16 parsecs that became famous when the first confirmed exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star (51 Peg b, now named Dimidium) was detected around it in 1995 by Mayor and Queloz. Named after the ancient Celtic inhabitants of Switzerland.
notable_features: 51 Pegasi, first Sun-like star with a confirmed exoplanet (1995)
Discovery of Dimidium (51 Peg b) won 2019 Nobel Prize in Physics

stars.name: Fomalhaut
stars.description: Alpha Piscis Austrini, the 18th brightest star in the sky at magnitude 1.17, an A3 dwarf at 7.7 parsecs. Its prominent debris disk was one of the first directly imaged, and its planet candidate Fomalhaut b generated controversy as either a genuine planet or a dust cloud.
notable_features: Prominent debris disk imaged by Hubble/JWST
One of the first stars with a directly imaged exoplanet candidate
Part of a wide triple star system

stars.name: Alfarasalkamil
stars.description: Omicron Andromedae, a B6 variable star at 211 parsecs in Andromeda. Its elaborate Arabic name means 'the complete horse.'

stars.name: Scheat
stars.description: Beta Pegasi, a semi-regular variable M2 giant at 60 parsecs forming the upper-right corner of the Great Square of Pegasus. Its diameter varies with its brightness over a period of about 43 days.

stars.name: Fumalsamakah
stars.description: Beta Piscium, a B6 emission-line star at 125 parsecs and the brightest star in Pisces. Its Arabic name means 'the mouth of the fish.'

stars.name: Markab
stars.description: Alpha Pegasi, a B9.5 giant at 41 parsecs forming the lower-right corner of the Great Square of Pegasus. It is a hot star nearing the end of its main-sequence life.

stars.name: Lacaille 9352
stars.description: An M2/M3 red dwarf at 3.3 parsecs in Piscis Austrinus, the fourth closest star system observable from Earth's southern hemisphere. It has the eighth-highest proper motion of all known stars.

stars.name: Ebla
stars.description: A K3 dwarf in Pisces at 29 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Syria after the ancient city. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Safina
stars.description: 88 Aquarii, a K1 giant at 83 parsecs in Aquarius. Its Arabic-derived name references a ship or boat.

stars.name: Honores
stars.description: 7 Andromedae, an F0 dwarf at 25 parsecs in Andromeda. Its Latin name means 'honors,' and it is one of the brighter non-Bayer stars in the constellation.

stars.name: Danfeng
stars.description: An M2 red dwarf in Tucana at 26 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by China. Despite its faintness, it is notable for hosting a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Salm
stars.description: Tau Pegasi, an A5 dwarf at 50 parsecs in the constellation Pegasus. It is a Delta Scuti variable with small rapid brightness oscillations.

stars.name: Alkarab
stars.description: Upsilon Pegasi, an F8 subgiant at 52 parsecs in Pegasus. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the drinking bucket's rope.'

stars.name: Veritate
stars.description: 14 Andromedae, a K0 giant at 79 parsecs hosting a confirmed exoplanet. Its Latin name meaning 'truth' was chosen through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign.

stars.name: Poerava
stars.description: An F7 dwarf in Tucana at 55 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by French Polynesia after a black pearl. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.

stars.name: Udkadua
stars.description: Lambda Andromedae, a G8 subgiant at 26 parsecs and an RS Canum Venaticorum variable with chromospheric activity. It is one of the closer active giant stars.

stars.name: Rasalnaqa
stars.description: Iota Andromedae, a B8 dwarf at 153 parsecs in Andromeda. Its Arabic name means 'the head of the she-camel.'

stars.name: Errai
stars.description: Gamma Cephei, a K1 subgiant at 14 parsecs hosting one of the first confirmed exoplanets. Due to precession, it will become the closest bright star to the north celestial pole around 3000 CE.

stars.name: Kaffalmusalsala
stars.description: Kappa Andromedae, a B9 subgiant at 52 parsecs directly imaged with a super-Jupiter companion. This massive companion straddles the boundary between giant planet and brown dwarf.

stars.name: Uúba
stars.description: Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign, this exoplanet host star carries a name meaning star in the Khoisan language of Botswana.

stars.name: Axólotl
stars.description: Named after the iconic Mexican salamander through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. This exoplanet host star represents Mexico.

stars.name: p Eridani
stars.description: A nearby K0 dwarf binary system at 7.8 parsecs in Eridanus. Both components are slightly cooler than the Sun and form a close pair resolvable in small telescopes.

stars.name: Castor B
stars.description: The A-type secondary component of the Castor sextuple system in Gemini at 16 parsecs. Together with Castor A, it forms the bright visual double resolvable in small telescopes.

stars.name: Kaewkosin
stars.description: A faint M-type star in Cancer at 23 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Thailand. It is one of the faintest named stars at magnitude 12.3.

stars.name: Maru
stars.description: A white dwarf of spectral type DQ5 in Volans at 23 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It is one of the very few white dwarfs with a proper name.

stars.name: Ross 686
stars.description: A faint M4 red dwarf at 25 parsecs in Hydra, cataloged by Frank Ross. It is a companion to the also-cataloged Ross 687 in the same field.

stars.name: Wolf 359
stars.description: One of the nearest stars to the Sun at 2.4 parsecs in Leo, an M6 red dwarf and one of the faintest known stars. Despite its fame in popular culture, it is far too dim to see without a telescope and is a known flare star.
notable_features: One of the faintest known stars

stars.name: Alula Australis
stars.description: Xi Ursae Majoris A, a G0 dwarf at 10 parsecs that was the first binary star to have its orbit calculated (by Felix Savary in 1827). Its name means 'the first leap (southern).'

stars.name: Alula Australis B
stars.description: The G0 dwarf companion to Xi Ursae Majoris A, forming the historic binary whose orbit was first computed. Both components are solar-type stars.

stars.name: Graffias
stars.description: Xi Scorpii, an F6 subgiant at 24 parsecs in Scorpius. Its Greek name means 'the crab' and it is a multiple star system.

stars.name: Alrakis
stars.description: Mu Draconis, a close binary of two F7 dwarfs at 23 parsecs in Draco. Both components are nearly identical, making it a challenging but rewarding telescopic target.

stars.name: Guniibuu B
stars.description: 36 Ophiuchi B, a K1 dwarf at 6.0 parsecs and the companion to the Guniibuu system. Together with its A component and a more distant C, it forms one of the nearest triple star systems.

stars.name: Onkaria
stars.description: A faint M-type red dwarf at 13 parsecs in Ophiuchus, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. At magnitude 14.7, it is one of the faintest stars with an IAU-approved name.

stars.name: EZ Aqr
stars.description: A triple red dwarf system at just 3.4 parsecs in Aquarius, one of the closest stellar systems to the Sun. All three components are M5 flare stars with very low luminosity.

stars.name: Ross 248
stars.description: A nearby M6 red dwarf at 3.2 parsecs in Andromeda, one of the closest stars to the Sun. Around the year 36,000 CE, it will temporarily become the nearest star to our Solar System as it passes within 0.9 parsecs.

stars.name: Tau Ceti
stars.description: A nearby G8.5 dwarf just 3.6 parsecs away in Cetus, long considered one of the most Sun-like stars. It hosts a multi-planet system with at least four candidate super-Earths, two of which may lie in the habitable zone.
notable_features: Multi-planet system with habitable zone candidates
One of the most Sun-like nearby stars

stars.name: Eta Carinae
stars.description: One of the most massive and luminous stars known, an unstable luminous blue variable ~2,300 parsecs away that underwent a spectacular eruption in the 1840s (the Great Eruption), briefly becoming the second-brightest star in the sky. It is surrounded by the Homunculus Nebula and is expected to explode as a supernova in the astronomically near future.
